summary: Paul Graham argues that Silicon Valley is reproducible elsewhere, but only if the right people move: a critical mass of nerds and startup investors. Buildings or government programs don’t create hubs; the key is a great university in an attractive, youthful town that retains founders and lets startup networks grow organically over time.
key_points: A startup hub requires both nerds and experienced, motivated investors; each alone is insufficient.
Government and bureaucracy can’t substitute for startup investors, and office parks don’t create startups.
Top-tier universities are magnets for talent and are necessary seeds for a hub.
Cities must have the right personality and appeal to young, odd-idea-tolerant nerds who want to stay.
Startup ecosystems grow via self-sustaining chains of founders and investors over time; there is no middle ground.
money_quotes: If you could get the right ten thousand people to move from Silicon Valley to Buffalo, Buffalo would become Silicon Valley.
I think you only need two kinds of people to create a technology hub: rich people and nerds.
You don't build a silicon valley; you let one grow.
Most good startup ideas seem a little crazy; if they were obviously good ideas, someone would have done them already.
Startups beget startups. People who work for startups start their own. People who get rich from startups fund new ones.
key_insight: To recreate Silicon Valley, cultivate a dense mix of top-tier technical talent and experienced investors in a livable, youthful city, then let a startup chain reaction grow organically over time.
